what_it_does: A front-end handoff document that specifies how to build the operator-facing authoring UX for cognition roles, rules, modes, and structured outputs on top of an already-built and acceptance-tested backend.
observations: The backend is described as built and verified on branch concurrent-cognition via bridge :8772, with 42 acceptance checks green in tests/authoring_acceptance.py.
It enumerates read endpoints (GET /api/cognition_info, SSE /api/stream) and write endpoints for roles (propose/edit/delete) and rules (validate/dry_run/attach/detach).
Role writes surface a proposal for operator approval via /api/resolve and /api/apply; nothing goes live without approval.
It defines data shapes: RoleFieldSpec, RuleAST, RuleDecl, the five destination_kinds, and cognition lifecycle event kinds.
It maps authoring affordances to existing FE surfaces (Palette, Inspector, NodeConfigForm, CognitionView, Inbox) and flags net-new components (rule-builder, schema editor, test/preview panel).

It lists eight laws the UI must honour, including reflects-never-owns, propose-not-apply, closed rule grammar, forbidden resolve/approve/dispatch destinations, registry-driven dropdowns, and fail-loud validation.
It includes backend cross-reference locations but contains no actual code symbols.
standouts: The backend is complete and acceptance-tested; the FE session is told to build only the UX, not the plumbing.
Every write is propose-not-apply: the operator must approve and apply before any role/rule becomes live.
Rules are a closed, depth-capped, deterministic data-AST with only five allowed destination kinds; resolve/approve/dispatch are explicitly forbidden.
preview_turn fires a real staged turn but is non-mutating to chat history/thread/training signal.
Authoring reuses the existing generic object_info/ui_info and NodeConfigForm rendering patterns.
conventions: Follows a structured handoff format with sections A through F.
Uses tables to map endpoints to request/response shapes.
Uses JSONc examples for verified live endpoint responses.
Reuses existing FE patterns rather than inventing new ones.
Insists on registry-driven, single-sourced dropdowns instead of hardcoded front-end lists.
concerns: The document is long and dense, mixing endpoint reference, UX design, and backend file pointers.
Net-new UX pieces such as the rule-builder and schema editor are described at a high level without concrete wireframes or detailed interaction states.
The directory-scan source node pairing is flagged as downstream but not fully specified.
notes: This is the FE build brief for the cognition authoring surface: the backend is ready, so the file maps exactly which UI to extend, what to build fresh, and the governance laws the surface must enforce.
questions: What should the rule-builder UI do when a rule exceeds MAX_RULE_DEPTH or references an unknown role?
How should the FE handle a brain-draft role/propose that returns needs because it hit unregistered ground?
What is the intended directory-scan source node pairing and where does its configuration live?
How should protected roles be visually distinguished in the palette and inspector?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to hand off FE authoring work, and the file does exactly that by documenting endpoints, data shapes, reusable surfaces, laws, and backend locations.
apparent_themes: Reflects-never-owns: backend is the single source of truth.
Cognition authoring as a composable node graph.
Reuse of generic registry-driven UI patterns.
intention_signals: The document is trying to enable a separate front-end session to implement the cognition authoring canvas without touching backend logic, while strictly enforcing governance, a closed rule language, and registry-driven UI.
novelty: It treats cognition authoring as the same node-graph substrate the app uses, with roles and rules as data-AST objects that render generically and must pass through a surfaced operator approval loop.
contribution: A comprehensive contract between backend and frontend that enumerates endpoints, data schemas, reusable UI surfaces, and non-negotiable laws, so a front-end implementation can be built against a verified backend without ambiguity.
summary_for_embedding: Authoring Surface — FE Handoff is a specification document mapping the already-built cognition authoring backend endpoints, data schemas, reusable front-end surfaces, and governance laws needed to implement the operator-facing role/rule authoring UX.
intention_for_embedding: This file was written to hand off front-end implementation of the cognition authoring surface to a separate FE session, ensuring it uses existing UI patterns and respects the propose-not-apply governance loop.
